title: Why submitting meter readings saves you money
description: Submitting regular meter readings stops your supplier estimating your bills, fixes a wrong direct debit and protects you when the price cap changes. Here is how.
blurb: Estimated bills guess your usage. A 30-second meter reading replaces the guess with the truth and keeps your direct debit honest.
lede: If you never send your supplier a meter reading, they do not stop billing you. They guess. Every estimated bill is the supplier's best assumption about how much gas and electricity you used, and an assumption is rarely right. Sometimes it bills you for energy you never burned and ties up money you could be using elsewhere. Sometimes it under-bills you, lets a debt build up quietly, then catches up with a shock bill months later. A meter reading takes about half a minute and replaces the guess with the actual number on your meter, which is the single most direct way to make sure you only pay for what you have used.
short_answer: Submitting a meter reading replaces your supplier's estimate with your real usage, so your bill and your monthly direct debit match what you have actually used. It stops you overpaying into a credit balance you cannot see, prevents a hidden debt building up, and stops you being charged old high-cap unit rates after the price cap has dropped. Aim to send a reading at least once a month, and always around a price-cap change date.

sections.h2: Why an estimated bill costs you money
sections.html:
When your supplier has no recent reading, it estimates your usage from your past consumption and from typical usage for a home like yours. That estimate then drives both your bill and your monthly direct debit. Neither is based on what you are really using right now.

An estimate that is too high means you are paying for energy you have not used. The money does not vanish, it sits as credit on your account, but it is your money parked with the supplier instead of in your bank. An estimate that is too low is worse in the long run. Your direct debit is set below your real cost, a debt builds up in the background, and eventually the supplier corrects it with a catch-up bill that can run to hundreds of pounds.

A reading ends both problems at once. The supplier reconciles your account to the actual figure, refunds or carries forward any genuine credit, and resets your direct debit to a realistic level. If you want to understand exactly which lines on your statement are estimated versus actual, our guide to understanding your energy bill breaks down what every figure means, and how to read your energy meter covers the dial, digital and smart meter types.

sections.h2: How much an estimate can be out by
sections.html:
The gap between an estimate and reality depends entirely on how wrong the guess is. Because you pay per kWh, a modest error in assumed usage turns into real money fast. The table shows what a given over-estimate or under-estimate looks like in pounds at current cap rates.

Estimate errorElectricity at 26.11p/kWhGas at 7.33p/kWh
500 kWh out£130.55£36.65
1,000 kWh out£261.10£73.30
2,000 kWh out£522.20£146.60

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

A typical home uses several thousand kWh of each fuel a year, so an estimate that drifts by even ten or fifteen percent over a few billing periods can leave you hundreds of pounds in credit or in debt without you ever seeing it coming. The standing charge is fixed at 57.19p a day for electricity and 29.04p a day for gas regardless of readings, so a reading only ever corrects the usage part. Our explainer on standing charges covers the part of the bill a reading cannot change.

sections.h2: Readings around a price-cap change matter most
sections.html:
The Ofgem price cap changes the unit rate every three months. The day the cap changes is the single most valuable day of the quarter to submit a reading, and the reason is simple. If you do not give a reading on or near the change date, your supplier splits your usage across the old and new rates using an estimate.

When the cap falls, an estimate can wrongly assume you used more energy during the more expensive period than you actually did, charging some of your usage at the old higher rate. When the cap rises, the same estimating can push usage you had already burned at the cheaper rate into the dearer band. A reading taken on the change date draws a hard line. Everything before it is billed at the old rate, everything after at the new one, with no guesswork in between.

Put a reminder in your phone for the start of January, April, July and October, the usual cap dates, and send a reading then. This matters whether you are on a capped variable deal or thinking about moving, and it pairs naturally with how the energy price cap works and switching suppliers, since you also want an accurate closing reading whenever you change tariff or provider.

sections.h2: Smart meters, and why readings still matter
sections.html:
A smart meter is meant to send readings automatically, which removes the chore for most people most of the time. That is the main reason it exists. But automatic does not mean foolproof. Smart meters drop off the network, lose their mobile signal, or fall back into estimating mode after a tariff change or a house move, and when that happens you are back to estimated bills without realising it.

The fix is to glance at your in-home display or your online account now and then. If recent statements say estimated rather than actual, send a manual reading even though the meter is smart. Many suppliers let you check whether your smart meter is sending data, and a manual reading overrides a stale estimate immediately. For the full picture on what these meters do and do not do, see smart meters explained.

If you are on a time-of-use deal such as Economy 7 or an EV tariff, accurate readings matter even more because your meter records two or more separate registers. Sending all of them keeps your cheap-rate and peak-rate usage correctly split. Our guides to Economy 7 and night rates and time-of-use tariffs explain how those registers work.

sections.h2: The back-billing rule that protects you
sections.html:
There is a safety net worth knowing about. Under Ofgem's rules, a supplier cannot bill you for energy used more than 12 months ago if the reason it was not billed properly is the supplier's fault, for example if it had your readings but estimated anyway, or it set your direct debit too low to cover your actual use.

This protection is real and you should claim it if a large catch-up bill lands for old usage. But it is a backstop, not a plan. The exception is where you actively prevented an accurate reading, for instance by blocking access to the meter. Submitting your own readings keeps your account current, removes the supplier's excuse to estimate, and means you almost never need the 12-month rule in the first place. Rules and protections can change, so check the current position on the official Ofgem page before relying on it. If a bill arrives you genuinely cannot pay, our guide on help if you cannot afford your energy bill sets out your options.

sections.h2: How and when to submit, the bottom line
sections.html:
Submitting a reading is the cheapest money-saving habit there is. It costs nothing, takes about 30 seconds, and keeps your bill anchored to reality instead of to a guess.

  • Read your meter on the same day each month, for example the 1st, and send it through your supplier's app, website or phone line.
  • Always send a reading on or near a price-cap change date and whenever you switch tariff or supplier.
  • Send a closing reading the day you move out of a property and an opening one the day you move in, so you are never billed for a previous occupant's usage.
  • If you have a smart meter, check a couple of times a year that statements say actual, not estimated, and send a manual reading if they do not.
  • Photograph the meter when you read it. A dated photo is your evidence if a bill is ever disputed.

If your bill or direct debit has jumped and you are not sure why, an up-to-date reading is the first thing to fix before anything else, and why did my direct debit go up walks through the other common causes.

faq.q: How often should I submit a meter reading?
faq.a: Once a month is a sensible rhythm, ideally on the same date each time so the gaps between readings are even. Beyond that, always send one on or near a price-cap change date and whenever you switch tariff or supplier, plus an opening and closing reading when you move home. If you have a working smart meter that sends data automatically, a monthly check that your statements say actual rather than estimated is enough.

faq.q: Will submitting a reading lower my bill?
faq.a: It does not change the unit rate you pay, so it will not make energy cheaper. What it does is make sure you are billed for the energy you actually used rather than an estimate. If your estimates have been too high you may move into credit or get a refund, and your direct debit can be reset to a lower, accurate level. If estimates have been too low, a reading prevents a debt building up that would otherwise hit you as a larger catch-up bill later.

faq.q: Why does a reading matter most when the price cap changes?
faq.a: When the cap changes, your supplier has to split your usage between the old rate and the new one. Without a reading on the change date it does this by estimating, which can wrongly bill cheaper-period usage at the dearer rate or vice versa. A reading taken on the change date draws a clean line, so everything before it is billed at the old rate and everything after at the new one.

faq.q: Can my supplier charge me for energy used over a year ago?
faq.a: Generally no, if the delay was the supplier's fault. Ofgem's back-billing rule stops suppliers charging for energy used more than 12 months ago where they failed to bill correctly, for example by estimating when they had your readings or by setting your direct debit too low. The protection does not apply if you actively prevented an accurate reading. Check the current rules on the Ofgem website, as they can change.

faq.q: Do I still need to read my meter if I have a smart meter?
faq.a: Most of the time no, because a working smart meter sends readings automatically. But smart meters can lose signal, drop off the network or fall back to estimating after a tariff change or house move. Glance at your in-home display or online account now and then, and if statements show estimated readings, send a manual one to override the estimate straight away.
